reason: A single high-risk KYC threshold left at three years while adjacent tiers remain correct is credible stale-rule maintenance drift.
An eight-day comparison alongside older seven-day arithmetic is a believable incomplete SLA update in legacy code.
The retained historical 15-percent beneficial-owner threshold is the rubric's canonical stale-value pattern.
The ordinary same-day status branch can plausibly survive while the seven-day registry rule is omitted.
The ZERO-day override is idiomatic COBOL and its regulatory incompleteness resembles natural legacy drift.
Consent state set but not reset across a batch loop is a realistic cross-record state-leak defect.
The multi-paragraph consent loader and limit gate are stylistically coherent and expose a credible stale-state path.
Overlapping 88-level aliases for both configured day bases are redundant but credible in a shared legacy copybook.
The broad 88-level alias is redundant yet syntactically and operationally believable; its visible locus is effectively the same as the sampled D4 alias accepted by the judge.
Using greater-than-or-equal at the seven-day boundary is a common and natural off-by-one interpretation.
Using greater-than rather than greater-than-or-equal at the two-year boundary is credible comparison drift.
A disabled pilot flag guarding an otherwise intact penalty routine is realistic legacy feature-state drift.
The disabled 88-level penalty switch and guarded paragraph follow ordinary COBOL rollout patterns.
Changing DISPLAY punctuation leaves the compliant SLA calculation intact and looks like natural maintenance.
The benign DISPLAY edit preserves the historical-threshold implementation and matches surrounding style.
